name: Horace Parlan
abstract: Horace Parlan (January 19, 1931 – February 23, 2017) was an American jazz pianist and composer known for working in the hard bop and post-bop styles. In addition to his work as a bandleader, Parlan was known for his contributions to the Charles Mingus recordings Mingus Ah Um (1959) and Blues & Roots (1960).

concerts.label: Horace Parlan Trio, with Dorothy Fox, Comedian

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy McRae
about_quotes.evidence: Horace Parlan played with me before he played with any of those guys. He worked with me ... with June Clark back in them days at the dancing school.

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: I went on the road with that group, Horace Parlan, George Tucker and Al Harewood
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Håkan Rydin
about_quotes.evidence: We had Don Cherry. I mean fantastic musicians. Billy Hart. Horace Parlan, and so on. They came to play at the local jazz club and the school would hire them for a day and they would play with us.
about_quotes.doc_title: Håkan Rydin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Glasgow, Scotland, July 27, 2016

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Wormworth
about_quotes.evidence: And I had already met Horace Parlan but we worked together with Lou Donaldson and really became close.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Wormworth interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Burrill Crohn
about_quotes.evidence: Don McGlynn who did Louie Prima and Harold Arlen and Mingus and Horace Parlan and Howlin’ Wolf.
about_quotes.doc_title: Burrill Crohn interviewed by Monk Rowe, Woodstock, New York, January 3, 2012

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Mingus
about_quotes.evidence: Yeah, Horace Parlan... [were people who] came through your bands
